data.title: Century Communities Offers Little Upside For Now (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The latest earnings season has been brutal for homebuilders. Century Communities, Inc. (NYSE:CCS) provided a small respite after the stock actually gained 4.8% on the first day of trading after reporting results for Q4 2024. Still, that gain was just more noise in the middle of a period of price indecision for the stock. Eventually, the weight of sobering news from other builders and poor housing data pushed CCS toward its lows of the year. Builders like Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (TPH) and Toll Brothers, Inc. (TOL) took big hits this week after disappointing investors. Moreover, builder sentiment plunged to a 5-month low. An extremely high 13-point drop in sentiment for 6-month sales expectations delivered a potentially ominous message for the spring selling season. An 8.4% sequential drop in housing starts, a 1.8% year-over-year decline, further reinforced a sobering outlook. Given this backdrop, tepid guidance from CCS for 10% order and delivery growth for 2025 and 2026 and declining margins suggest the stock will have minimal upside in the near term. Accordingly, I downgraded CCS to neutral.

Slowing Business Pace

CCS set a number of corporate records in Q4 2024 and for the full year. From the earnings conference call transcript:

  • Full-year record deliveries of 11,007 homes (up 15% year-over-year).
  • Community count of 322 (up 28% year-over-year).
  • Quarterly record deliveries of 3,198 homes (up 1.4% year-over-year).
  • Quarterly record home sales revenues of $1.2B (up 5% year-over-year).

These records are the result of managing the company for incremental growth quarter-by-quarter.

Yet, the guidance indicates a slowdown in business performance. Despite a 28% growth in communities and an expected future growth percentage in the mid to high single-digit range, CCS only expects a 9.5% growth in deliveries for 2025 (at the midpoint of an 11,700 to 12,400 range) and 2026, significantly down from 2024’s 15% growth. The high side of guidance holds out the prospect for 12.7% growth, and the low side cautions growth could be as low as 6.3%. Expected home sales revenue of $4.5B to $4.8B is just 5.7% higher at the midpoint than 2024’s full-year revenue of $4.4B.

This growth slowdown comes with expected absorption flat-lining at a rate of 3.2. The company is managing a delicate balance between affordability of its homes, sales pace, and margins. Incentives sit at the center of these tensions. While full-year margins increased slightly by 80 basis points to 23.3%, margin performance in Q4 dragged on results. For Q3, CCS recorded an adjusted gross margin of 23.6% which declined to 22.9% in Q4. The company anticipates further weakness in Q1 2025 even as SG&A falls as a percent of revenue. Incentives on closed homes in Q4 increased to 800 basis points from 700 basis points in Q3 and climbed to 900 basis points for Q4 orders. CCS did not provide full year guidance margins but did acknowledge that “for the first quarter 2025, we expect our homebuilding gross margin to ease on a sequential basis.”

A graph of TTM (trailing twelve month) revenue vs. EPS vs. gross profit shows in stark relief CCS’s struggle for profitable growth. While revenue is finally climbing back to the peak in 2022, both gross profit and EPS remain well off their peaks. Moreover, the recent uptrend in revenue has diverged from gross profit and a relatively flat EPS profile.

Note Century Communities is also trimming its footprint ever so slightly to meet affordability needs. The company reported a 2% year-over-year decrease in overall square footage for 2024. ASP (average selling price) still increased 4% for the year.

Valuation

Valuation is always the temptress for homebuilders. Valuation is typically low but can go even lower. CCS sports valuation metrics below both sector median and its own 5-year average. Most telling is a price/book ratio below 1.0 which means the stock is priced for recession. The 0.9 TTM and 0.8 forward price/book ratios are both well below CCS’s 5-year averages of 1.1 and 1.0 respectively. The sector medians are more than double these levels. While price/sales are around CCS’s 5-year average, they are roughly half the sector median. The price/earnings rations (GAAP) are the same story. Thus, while CCS has a tempting low valuation, it is not trading at a sufficient discount to itself to warrant a buy hoping for an upside surprise later in the year.

Conclusion

The stakes are high for CCS in 2025 after significantly underperforming the iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF in 2024 a 19.5% loss on the year versus ITB’s 1.8% gain. Since the end of the trough year 2022, CCS is up 44.5% versus ITB’s 65.8%. Year-to-date, CCS has avoided new lows unlike ITB which is down 2.8% year-to-date. CCS is down 1.5%. If it can hold the lows for support, it has a decent chance to to out-perform ITB for the rest of the year. Even for the seasonal trade on homebuilders, the technicals do not favor a speculative buy on CCS until at least a breakout above the down trending 50-day moving average (DMA) (the red line below). This trendline has capped CCS since the October earnings report.

So while CCS looks relatively cheap to its peers, it is not cheap enough relative to itself to warrant the risk for what looks like minimal upside in the near-term. The slowing growth profile and declining margins (mainly due to affordability efforts) augur poorly for the critical spring selling season and puts growth guidance for 2026 at risk.

Be careful out there!

data.title: Allspring Disciplined U.S. Core Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) gained 2.41% during the fourth quarter of 2024. Led once again by mega-cap, technology-focused companies, U.S. equities moved higher over the removal of the U.S. election uncertainty, solid economic growth, lower rates, healthy corporate earnings, and expectations of a more business-friendly regulatory environment. West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices rose 5%, U.S. large caps outperformed small caps, and growth outperformed value.

Persistently high shelter costs pushed November’s annualized rate of U.S. headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) inflation back up to +2.7%, while annualized core CPI inflation held steady at 3.3%. New U.S. job growth rebounded in November, while the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.2%. U.S. annualized real gross domestic product (GDP) expanded by 3.1% during the third quarter of 2024. The consensus estimates for fourth quarter real GDP growth are now ~3%, reflecting growing confidence in the health of the U.S. economy.

In December, the Federal Open Market Committee reduced the federal funds rate by another 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), down to a target range of between 4.25% and 4.50%. However, in an increasingly hawkish shift, the U.S. central bank projected only two additional 25-bp rate cuts in 2025, lowering the federal funds rate to approximately 3.90% by the end of 2025.

Figures quoted represent past performance, which is noguarantee of future results, and donotreflect taxes a shareholder maypay on an investment in a fund. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance maybe lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.51%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.48%. The manager has contractually committed, through November 30, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.48% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Portfolio characteristics include: 130–220 holdings, a target sector weight +/-1% relative to the benchmark, and identification of attractively valued companies with improving earnings fundamentals relative to the benchmark.

The fund returned 4.18% for the quarter, while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned 2.41%. The fund’s outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within IT, industrials, and health care. Negative stock selection within utilities and energy detracted from performance. The alpha model results were positive for the quarter. The momentum and quality factor groups showed strength, while value was also positive. From a risk factor perspective, the strategy’s positive exposures to beta and momentum contributed to performance. The strategy’s positive exposures to earnings yield, value, and profitability and a negative exposure to residual volatility detracted from performance. These relative risk exposures were modest. During the quarter, the strategy rotated into IT and consumer staples and away from communication services and health care stocks. Within IT, we added to hardware and software companies that should benefit as the artificial intelligence trade rotates from the earlier gains in semiconductors. We added to lower-beta consumer staples and trimmed higher-beta biotechnology companies to maintain beta 1 as higher-beta names performed strongly during the quarter. Within financials, the strategy rotated from financial services companies to insurance stocks that trade at more attractive multiples and typically benefit from lower interest rates and a less restrictive regulatory environment.

Portfolio Positioning: Trailing 12-Month Attribution Analysis

Over the past 12 months, the fund returned 29.72% while the S&P 500 Index returned 25.02%. The outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within IT, industrials, and financials. Negative stock selection within real estate and energy detracted from relative performance. Over the longer-term, broad diversification and risk controls surrounding sector weights have typically resulted in stock selection being the main driver of performance within the strategy. Regarding macroeconomic risks, the portfolio has maintained tight exposure to inflation shocks and oil as a means of weathering the volatility in commodities and geopolitical risks. Despite the Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) reduction of short-term interest rates by 75 bps since September and expectations for two additional cuts in 2025, long-term rates have risen nearly 100 bps since the Fed’s rate cutting cycle began. Accounting for the potential risks and volatility in interest rates, the strategy has maintained neutral exposure to long and short interest rates. The portfolio was also neutral to credit spreads, which fell substantially during 2024 and now sit at levels not seen since 2005. Our experience demonstrates that fundamentally weighted strategies that invest in relatively inexpensive companies with improving fundamentals typically reward long-term investors. We continue to build portfolios that adhere to this philosophy while recognizing that the current pervasive macroeconomic risks require an elevated emphasis on risk controls, particularly as they relate to industry exposures, secular growth, oil prices, inflation expectations, and credit risk. We still prefer an emphasis on secular value (i.e., quality and value) over risk and beta, as we continue through the later phases of the economic cycle.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

As we enter 2025, it appears the U.S. has achieved a soft economic landing. Continued disinflation, lower interest rates, and a strong labor market have eliminated most near-term U.S. recession risks. In September 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates for the first time since the 2020 onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, concluding the Fed’s fastest tightening cycle in more than four decades. However, the persistence of inflation, strong economic growth, and low unemployment should force the U.S. central bank to take a more measured approach in 2025. Despite the looming threat of a tariff war by the incoming Trump administration, U.S. GDP growth will likely continue outperforming other developed markets, as monetary policy may shift rapidly from a significant headwind into an economic tailwind.

Since mid-July, equity market returns have broadened out from the “Magnificent Seven” (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla), as the “other 493 stocks” have performed relatively well. After the concentrated mega-cap rally of 2023 and throughout most of 2024, equity markets in 2025 are likely to be defined by slowing global economic growth, single-digit earnings growth, and a broadening of returns. We think that volatility is likely amid a looming tariff war and ongoing geopolitical tensions surrounding Ukraine, the Middle East, and Taiwan. Given this backdrop, we expect 2025 to provide opportunities for disciplined stock selection to add value as it did during 2024. We currently anticipate volatile and range-bound markets and are mindful to neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are generally cautious, there are potential positive catalysts from decelerating inflation, lower rates, a resilient U.S. job market, improving corporate earnings, and a broadening of market returns.

data.title: Agree Realty: When The Perfect Blend Of Consistency, Reliability, And Quality Meet
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to Agree Realty (NYSE:ADC), the REIT has all the makings of an obvious long-term hold. Although REITs have been somewhat disappointing investments lately due to macro environmental headwinds, long-term income investors should be looking to the sector for value.

What also makes Agree Realty a great investment is management's Quality over Quantity embodiment. ADC takes pride in their strong portfolio consisting of the biggest and best retailers in the country.

I also enjoy the fact that management never waivers nor deviates from their core investment strategy of acquiring high-quality properties and minimizing the possibility of delivering mediocre returns.

Moreover, their limited susceptibility to economic downturns as a result of their high-exposure to recession-resistant tenants and strong growth runway, gives the stock significant upside over the long term in my opinion.

In this article, I discuss Agree Realty's latest earnings, financials, and why this high quality REIT is a must-own in an income-focused portfolio despite double-digit price appreciation in the past year.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Agree Realty this past October in an article titled: Agree Realty: Quality Always Rises To The Top. At a then forward P/AFFO multiple of 17.86x, ADC offered significant upside potential as they traded well-below their 5-year average of 20.14x.

As stated in the article, I suspected Agree Realty could reach its 5-year average or above over the next 12 to 24 months. This gave the REIT double-digit upside to a price target of $86.

Since then, inflation worries have kept the stock's share price relatively flat, down 3.66% in comparison to the S&P who is in the green by an almost identical amount, 3.68%.

ADC's Q3 earnings saw them raise full-year guidance again, this time to a range of $4.12 - $4.14. And despite core FFO declining from the previous quarter, this along with their revenue grew solidly year-over-year. The REIT's balance sheet and strong acquisitions also allowed them to raise their acquisition and disposition guidance for the year.

Q4 & Full-Year Performance

ADC's fourth quarter and full-year performance again showed why they remain one of the best net lease REITs to own. During earnings, the REIT beat on both their top and bottom lines, a further testament to their high-quality considering the challenging economic backdrop.

AFFO of $1.04 managed to beat analysts' estimates by $0.02 while revenue of $160.73 million beat by a solid $1.58 million. This brought their full-year AFFO right at the top of management's guidance range of $4.14. This resulted in a 4.8% increase year-over-year from 2023's $3.95.

For comparison purposes, peer NNN REIT (NNN) saw a 2.8% increase in AFFO year-over-year to $3.35 a share.

During the quarter, AFFO increased 4% from $1.00 in the prior year's quarter. In Q4, their strong growth continued as the REIT invested $371 million in 127 net lease properties. This was higher than the previous quarter that saw management invest $237 million across 90 properties.

And brought ADC's total property count to 2,370 properties, up from 2,271. Year-over-year, their property count grew from 2,135 properties. Considering their strong growth from the previous year, ADC's growth runway remains long, with less than 2,500 properties.

And this doesn't even include the fact they haven't tapped into the international space yet, which could come much further in the future.

For the year, they invested $951 million in 282 properties and commenced development on an additional 25 properties worth $115 million. For the quarter, acquisitions amounted to roughly $342 million in 98 properties, acquired at a weighted-average cap rate of 7.3%, slightly higher than last year that saw a weighted-average cap rate of 7.2%.

Acquisitions for the full-year totaled $866 million, exceeding the $850 million set in Q3.

Dispositions in the quarter totaled 8 properties and 26 properties for the full-year at a WACR of 6.7%, resulting in 80 basis points investment spreads and generating robust accretion for shareholders. For the year, dispositions netted the REIT $98.4 million in proceeds.

2025 Guidance & Long-Term Position

Another metric that makes Agree Realty the top net lease REIT that is likely to drive strong returns for shareholders is the company's fortress of a balance sheet. Over the last several months, management took advantage of the sector rally in July by selling shares, further strengthening their liquidity, positioning the REIT well for 2025 and beyond.

Their net debt to EBITDA was a strong 4.9x and 3.3x on a pro forma basis. This is in comparison to NNN's 5.5x. ADC's minimal debt maturities and robust liquidity that stood at $2 billion further solidifies why they're likely poised for further upside.

This allows the REIT the flexibility to continue to see solid growth, both externally and internally. They also extended their revolver to $1.25 billion and maturity to August of 2029. And as Agree Realty continues to acquire properties and see tailwinds from lower interest rates, the REIT is likely to see strong price appreciation over the long term.

Management issued 2025 AFFO guidance of $4.26 - $4.30, representing a solid 3.38% at midpoint. But like 2024, I expect the goal post to move to the right for ADC. Additionally, acquisitions are expected to be between $1.1 billion to $1.3 billion for the year and disposition volume in a range of $10 million to $50 million.

Well-Covered Monthly Dividend

Shareholders also get paid a well-covered monthly dividend while they wait for potential upside, which I see plenty over the next 12 to 24 months. ADC ended the year with an AFFO payout ratio of 73.3%. This was in comparison to NNN's AFFO payout ratio of 68.2%.

However, both were very conservative for REITs in general. And this gives Agree Realty ample room for consistent and steady dividend increases, which I suspect will continue for the foreseeable future. The REIT was also approaching $100 million in retained income after dividends, giving them additional capital for potential M&A activity.

Ground Lease Portfolio

Agree's hidden gem is their ground lease portfolio, which has even higher exposure to investment-grade tenants than their net lease property portfolio. During the quarter, the REIT acquired an additional 6 properties to bring their total to 229 across 36 states.

And I expect management to focus on growing this for the foreseeable future. At the end of the year, the portfolio was 100% occupied with 88.2% exposure to investment-rated tenants.

Upside Potential/Risks

Although Agree Realty trades at a higher premium in comparison to many of its peers, I think the REIT is poised to see strong upside as the macro environmental picture becomes clearer.

Additionally, their forward multiple of 16.58x is well-below their 5-year average and not much higher than the sector median's 16.10x. If interest rates decline this year and going forward, I expect ADC could trade somewhere in between their regular and high price target of $89.

However, the hotter than expected recent CPI report could result in higher for longer interest rates. And if they do remain steady for 2025, this will result in limited upside for Agree Realty as volatility will likely pick up in treasuries.

Some economists are even calling for a rate hike as early as this summer. Although I think that scenario is unlikely, surging inflation in the future could force the FED's hand. If so, this would most certainly result in downside for Agree Realty as well as the entire sector.

Bottom Line

With a forward P/E of 16.58x using 2025's guidance midpoint, Agree Realty trades significantly below their 5-year average. Quality usually doesn't come cheap, and the recent pullback from their 52-week high in recent months should be viewed as a long-term buying opportunity.

With a perfect blend of quality, reliability, and consistency, Agree Realty is the perfect stock to put inside of an income-focused portfolio that could help further grow your wealth. As a result of this and their strong runway for growth, I continue to rate Agree Realty a buy.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: The Cheapest Great AI Server Provider You Can Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (NYSE:HPE) is one of the world's leading IT enterprise solution providers. The company is known for its servers, storage, networking, and software solutions that enterprises need to enable their IT infrastructure. With the rise of AI, the company is strategically refocusing on this industry as it can provide the growth that can propel the company out of stagnation.

Based on what I've seen in HPE's AI Day presentation, I believe that they have a sound strategy to capitalize on the growing AI industry by accelerating enterprise AI adoption. Additionally, HPE's integrated solutions, from financing, storage, computing, and networking to software, will play a crucial role in its strategy to accelerate AI adoption among enterprises. With HPE being the cheapest great AI server provider stock you can buy right now, I believe this presents a wonderful opportunity for investors, especially value-seeking ones, who are hesitant to invest in AI right now due to how expensive most of the stocks participating in this area are. Thus, I will call a 'Buy' rating for this HPE.

Accelerating the Enterprise AI Adoption

In HPE's AI Day presentation, the management identified key growth areas with which they believe the company has an opportunity.

In their presentation, the AI market is segmented into four categories: Hyperscalers, Service Providers, Sovereigns, and Enterprises. While the whole market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 25%, HPE's real opportunity will be in the sovereign and enterprises. However, there are opportunities among model builders and AI service providers.

Hyperscalers & Service Providers

Hyperscalers are big cloud players like Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) that build their own AI infrastructure to train AI models. HPE has limited opportunity on traditional hyperscalers, as they typically source their AI servers through Original Direct Manufacturers or ODMs. The real opportunities are among the smaller model builders, whose AI models are trained for specialized needs such as healthcare, law, cybersecurity, and more. These customers are usually small start-ups with limited access to capital that can benefit from HPE's pay-as-you-go model. This circumvents the huge CAPEX usually associated with AI infrastructure buildouts.

HPE also sees opportunities among AI service providers. Service providers enable customers to deliver value from their AI investments and can add HPE's infrastructure expertise to a turnkey solution that their customers usually seek. Service providers play a crucial role in HPE's enterprise AI strategy as they possess differentiated value-adding activities that enterprises, businesses, and model-builders can use to realize value from AI.

Additionally, HPE's integrated solution, which includes storage, networking, and servers, will be well received by these customers as it simplifies the infrastructure buildout process. They can focus less on the infrastructure and more on running their businesses.

Sovereign & Enterprise

HPE's biggest AI opportunity will be in this segment as it plays to its years of experience in enabling hybrid cloud implementation for enterprise and sovereign customers. They also have existing customer relations in this segment that they can leverage to accelerate their enterprise AI strategy. With the help of AI service partners, HPE can provide turnkey AI solutions for enterprises seeking to integrate AI into their operations easily. Due to a lack of AI expertise, partly due to the shortage of AI talents, enterprises are more inclined to pay a premium for these complete AI solutions, thus providing an accretive margin opportunity for HPE. The HPE's traditional pay-as-you-go model will be a significant value proposition for enterprises as they prefer to avoid huge upfront CAPEX investments, especially for a technology whose value creation capability for a business is still unproven or uncertain.

Additionally, HPE's scalable solution will allow enterprises to run their proofs-of-concept as cheaply as possible and easily scale once pushed into production. This can be a significant value proposition as there is still a lot of uncertainty regarding AI use cases among enterprises. The companies that can clear the fog in AI towards a value-creating outcome through their comprehensive solution will have a significant advantage over others that only provide part of the solution. I believe that HPE will be one of those companies.

Rising Deferred Revenue

One of the best measures for companies like HPE, due to its service products and long-term pay-as-you-go arrangement with its Greenlake solution, is its deferred revenue.

Since 2018, HPE's deferred revenue has been stagnant, which indicates a lack of interest in HPE's products and services. This can be attributed to the industry's maturity and intense competition from Dell (DELL) and Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY). However, the rise of AI in 2023 altered this trend and provided a catalyst for the entire industry to grow. Since 2023, HPE has witnessed significant growth in its deferred revenue, which indicates a growing demand for its products and services; it can be an early indicator of the company's future growth. Additionally, rising deferred revenue growth indicates whether HPE's strategy is gaining traction or not. Thus, it is one important metric we should monitor moving forward.

HPE's Acquisition Of Juniper Will Strengthen Their Enterprise AI Portfolio

Last year, HPE announced its intention to acquire Juniper Networks (JNPR). HPE management believes that Juniper's product portfolio will strengthen its enterprise networking portfolio. HPE can gain access to Juniper's high-performance networking solution to strengthen its AI infrastructure solution for enterprise customers. There are also synergistic benefits as both companies gain access to each other's customers, effectively increasing the total addressable market of HPE and Juniper. I also believe that the integration effort, which has been the bane of many corporate M&A failures in the past, will be significantly easier because of the similarity of both businesses and the complementary nature of both businesses towards each other. And based on the management's rationale for the acquisition, this seems to be the case.

Before I pass it over to Rami, which is here with me today, I would like to quickly stress that is also make a strategic sense as it aligns two complementary businesses with similar cultures. It is a natural fit. HPE and Juniper have a similar commitment to innovation and relentless focus on our customers. And Juniper has got an incredible strong team. And we're looking forward to bringing our two companies together.

Interpreting what management is saying here, it seems that the intention for the acquisition is more for Juniper's assets: its customer relationships, product portfolio, assets, and distribution channel. Integration of this type is far easier because of the similarity of the value and process that converts input into output. Acquisitions are typically a disaster if a company is integrating another company whose culture, value, and processes are totally misaligned with the acquirer. For example, integrating a discount store into an upscale store will be a disaster due to the significant difference in the processes, values, and cultures that guide both businesses to excel in their respective markets. The logical choice for this kind of acquisition is to give the acquired company autonomy. In HPE's case, I believe plug-in integration will be logical. You integrate the ones you need and remove the redundant ones.

I believe HPE's acquisition of Juniper will deliver value for the shareholders in the long term due to the clear synergistic benefit and ease of integration. The acquisition is also manageable from a financial standpoint.

On a pro forma basis, the combined EBITDA of both companies will generate around $5.67B, around 3.5x of HPE's current debt, which is manageable. It is still close to Dell's current Debt/EBITDA leverage ratio of around 2.8x. Please note that I divide the $450M cost synergy that the management believes will be achievable within 36 months by three($450M / 3 years = $150M cost savings per year).

This deal still faces regulatory hurdles, but I believe that the DOJ's argument is weak due to how competitive the enterprise WLAN market is. This market will still have multiple competitors even if HPE and Juniper merge. I'm inclined that this deal will go through.

Valuation

The good thing about investing in AI is that regardless of a firm's strategy, everyone can succeed in this growing industry as long as the company imitates what others are doing successfully. This is until the industry matures, cutthroat competition takes over, and everyone's profitability goes down the drain. But AI is still a young industry, so we don't need to worry about it for a while. Of course, strategy matters, and you can grow quicker than the industry average if you have the right strategy. Considering the AI tailwinds, I believe that the best method to valuate HPE is its earnings yield and how it compares to its competitors. I will calculate earnings yield by simply dividing HPE's current EBIT by its current enterprise value.

With an earnings yield of 7.34%, you get exposure to the high-growth potential of the AI industry. Compared to an alternative no-growth 4.5% risk-free 10-year US bond yield, I believe buying HPE is a much better deal and worth the risk. Therefore, I will call a 'Buy' for this stock. Why just a 'Buy' rating? Because, as a value investor, I prefer a lower price tag.

Risk

Enterprise Adoption

The investment thesis laid out in this article heavily relies on the adoption of AI among enterprises. Enterprises are still struggling to integrate AI into their business operations, and there is still uncertainty about the return on investments with AI. The anticipated adoption of AI in enterprises presented in this article may not happen or take longer than expected, which could affect HPE's valuation. Despite the clear and significant hurdle of how enterprises will integrate AI into their operations, this also presents an opportunity for HPE. Enterprises overcoming this hurdle with HPE will create customer relationships that can be beneficial down the road due to reciprocation.

Competition

HPE also faces intense competition in all segments presented in their AI Day. Original direct manufacturers are the typical go-to solution for hyperscalers due to their specialized AI server designs. In the sovereign and enterprises, HPE faced intense competition from Dell and Lenovo, which all have similar business models. They offer similar products, a pay-as-you-go model, and turnkey solutions, and they also have relationships with the same AI service providers. Hyperscalers also compete with HPE's hybrid cloud business model. Enterprises may choose to run their AI workload in the cloud instead of HPE's private cloud. However, the good thing is that AI is a high-growth industry. It presents an opportunity for all its participants to achieve respectable growth without aggravating competition, resulting in cutthroat competition that can impede everyone's growth and profitability.

Manager Commentary

“Developed markets outperformed emerging markets, as U.S. equities strengthened amid prospects of deregulation in the country following the elections.” - Acadian Asset Management LLC

Market in Review

During the fourth quarter of 2024, global markets added to their 2024 positive performance, rising 1.9%. Gains were supported by falling inflation and the ensuing rate cuts by major central banks. Both the U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) and the European Central Bank (“ECB”) reduced rates twice during the quarter. However, uncertainties around the incoming Trump administration’s trade policies weighed on markets, paring back some of those gains. Developed markets outperformed emerging markets, as U.S. equities strengthened amid prospects of deregulation in the country following the elections. At the same time, emerging markets suffered due to anticipation of more protectionist policies in the U.S., with China and India leading these losses. Japanese stocks were a standout, outperforming all the major economies in the period. The Bank of Japan, however, decided to tread cautiously on rate hikes amid uncertainty around Trump’s future economic policies.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor International Core Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned -4.31%, outperforming its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE (ND) Index, which returned - 8.11%.

Stock selection within countries contributed to return, while country allocations also contributed positively. Key sources of positive active return included stock selection in Sweden, a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in Italy, and stock selection in the United Kingdom. Detractors from return included opportunistic exposures to South Korea and Brazil, and a combination of stock selection and an underweight position in Singapore.

From a sector perspective, key sources of positive active returns included a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in Information Technology, a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in Industrials, and a combination of stock selection and an underweight position in Health Care.

Contributors and Detractors

During the quarter, Spotify Technology (SPOT) and Wix.com (WIX) contributed to returns. Our proprietary forecast for Spotify showed attractiveness across all characteristics within our bottom-up stock selection model, aside from value, which was marginally negative. Furthermore, our top-down and peer models contributed to alpha. Our overweight position to this stock was beneficial, as it was up 21.40% during the quarter.

During the quarter, our underweight position in Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG) detracted from returns, as did Experian (OTCQX:EXPGF).

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Naver (OTCPK:NHNCF), a South Korean internet conglomerate. At the time of purchase, within our bottom-up stock selection model — in order of magnitude — the technical, growth, quality, and value signals were attractive. Meanwhile, the peer model contributed to alpha scores. Within our top-down model, the industry outlook added to the stock’s forecast.

Purchase decisions are made in conjunction with sell decisions; the process is completely objective, automated, and driven by changes in expected returns. After computing the forecast returns, the investment process ranks all securities in Acadian’s universe, determining the most attractive securities to buy for the Fund based on investment guidelines, as well as transactional and trading liquidity considerations. For a stock to be purchased, it must have a more attractive alpha forecast or diversification properties (including a hurdle for estimated transaction costs) than an existing holding and other potential holdings. Essentially, if a stock is more attractive than an existing holding, allowing for the transaction costs of both the sell and the buy, it will be bought.

During the quarter, we sold MS&AD Insurance Group Holdings (OTCPK:MSADF). At the time of sale, within our bottom-up stock selection model — in order magnitude — growth and technical signals were unattractive. Meanwhile, the peer model contributed to alpha scores. Within our top-down model, the industry outlook added to the stock’s forecast.

We will automatically sell a stock if its expected return deteriorates to the point where it can be replaced by a more attractive stock that plays an equally useful or more attractive role in the Fund. Importantly, since markets are not frictionless, the expected excess return of any stock added to the Fund must more than cover the expected transaction costs of selling the original position and buying the new stock.

Sector Underweights and Overweights

Entering the fourth quarter, the Fund continued to be overweight in the Information Technology and Industrials sectors. The Information Technology overweight was mainly concentrated in Japan and Israel. The Industrials overweight was a combination of positioning in Switzerland and Italy. The top two sector underweights were Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary. The underweight in Consumer Staples was concentrated mainly in the United Kingdom and Switzerland, whereas the underweight in Consumer Discretionary was driven by positioning in Japan and France.

There were no material changes to sector or industry allocations during the quarter. Marginal positive shifts across sectors included Information Technology, Communication Services, and Industrials, whereas the largest negative shift was out of Health Care. Given Acadian's quantitative investment process and the bottom-up nature of our Fund construction methods, the Fund changes gradually based on the attractiveness or unattractiveness of individual holdings. We do not make changes based on a view of the overall investment environment. Acadian seeks the best stock selection opportunities for the Fund, subject to country, sector, and risk limits intended to maximize active returns from selection rather than any one specific factor.

As of quarter-end, the Fund continues to be overweight in Information Technology and Industrials. The Information Technology overweight is mainly concentrated in Japan and Israel. The Industrials overweight is a combination of positioning in Switzerland and Netherlands. The top two sector underweights were Consumer Staples and Health Care. The underweight in Consumer Staples is concentrated mainly in the United Kingdom and Switzerland, whereas the underweight in Health Care is driven by positioning in France and United Kingdom.

Country Allocation

Country and sector weights for the Fund are a product of the bottom-up stock selection process, combined with the risk controls in the optimization process. Bottom-up stock selection continued to drive the Fund.

During the quarter, relative to the benchmark, the Fund saw proportionally more assets flow into Germany and Australia. Opportunistic exposure increased in South Korea. Negative asset flow was seen in Italy, Denmark, and Spain.

As of quarter-end, in terms of country positioning, the largest overweight positions were in Israel and Switzerland. The Fund had notable opportunistic exposure to China and Taiwan. The biggest underweight positions were in the United Kingdom, Japan, and France.

In terms of sector positioning, the focus was on Information Technology, Communication Services, and Industrials. The biggest underweight positions were in Consumer Staples, Health Care, and Materials.

The Fund’s exposure to developing markets did not materially change during the quarter.

Outlook

The Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (“OECD”) believes the global economy will remain resilient in the face of macroeconomic challenges in 2025. Economic growth is likely to stabilize. Concerns over interest rates decreased with central banks cutting rates amid falling inflation, which is now back to central bank targets in most major economies. Labor markets have also eased, although unemployment rates remain near historical lows. The OECD raised its growth outlook for the global economy recently, estimating growth of 3.2% in 2024 and expecting the global economy to grow 3.3% year over year, both in 2025 and 2026.

Meanwhile, inflation in the OECD economies is expected to ease further to 3.8% in 2025 and 3.0% in 2026 — from 5.4% in 2024 — as monetary policies remain restrictive in most economies. Headline inflation is likely to continue falling, led by significant declines in food, energy, and goods price inflation.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (“EIA”) expects global oil production to increase by 1.6 million barrels per day in 2025, close to 90% of which is expected to be from non-OPEC+ countries. This is due to the continued production restraint announced by OPEC+. At its December 5 meeting, the body announced it would delay production increases until April 2025. The EIA expects the Brent Crude oil spot price to average $74/b in 2025, as oil markets are anticipated to remain balanced. However, persistent geopolitical tensions, particularly in the Middle East, remain a potential risk.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund’s current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund’s total annual operating expenses. The net expense ratios for this fund are subject to a contractual management fee waiver and/or expense limitation agreement, excluding interest expense and acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), through 02/28/2025.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Past performance reflects the beneficial effect of any expense waivers or reimbursements, without which returns would have been lower. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at Active ETFs or by calling 800-422-1050.

Disclosures

All holdings-related data is provided by FactSet. Because FactSet relies on external sources for its data, that data may differ slightly from actual values maintained by Harbor Funds.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Beta is a rolling three year, unless the Fund has a track record of less than three years, in which case it is a rolling one year. Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Alpha is a measure of risk (beta) adjusted return.

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward-looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MKOR: Decent Credentials And Potential To Recover
data.description:
Picking Up The Baton From MAKOX At More Competitive Costs

The landscape of actively managed “ETFs” with a geographic focus on Korea isn’t littered with a plethora of options; the Matthews Korea Active ETF (NYSEARCA:MKOR), with less than $50m in total AUM, is currently one of the handful of geographically themed active ETFs that make up this developing terrain. As the name suggests, MKOR offers active coverage to a narrow pool of stocks based in South Korea (both common and preferred). However, what’s interesting to note is that while MKOR only got listed as an ETF in July 2023, it is essentially carrying the baton from the now defunct Matthews Korea Fund (MAKOX), which had been around from October 2010 until July 2023. MKOR has since picked up the assets and liabilities of MAKOX, and continues to operate under similar investing principles, albeit at a much more competitive cost for investors (the expense ratio of the fund was 1.13%, but MKOR’s expense ratio is priced a lot lower at just 0.79%). So, what does that constitute?

Well, they seek to leverage fundamental bottoms-up analysis encompassing the strength of the balance sheet, cash flow sustainability, general financial health, quality of the management, product lines, corporate governance, marketing strategies, etc. that eventually lead to Korean stocks capable of sustainable growth over time.

Was the active management particularly helpful before conversion to the ETF structure? Well, if we measure it against the most popular Korean product - the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) - you’ll see that its average annual returns per year (5.4%) before conversion were superior to the latter (4.3%). Having said that, we also recognize that some investors may be perturbed by the degree of losses in a couple of bad years (2018 and 2022).

Matthews Korea In Its New Avatar vs iShares MSCI Korea ETF

Since 2023, MKOR has been run by two lead managers - Elli Lee and Sojung Park - who have honed their stock picking skills for roughly 8-9 years at Matthews Asia. They are also ably supported by Michael Oh, who served as co-manager, and appears to be a lot more entrenched in this business, having been around with Matthews Asia for over 25 years! So far, in the ETF cloak, their efforts appear to be paying off; Korean stocks in general have been on a slump since the second half of 2021 but MKOR is still outperforming EWY by over 100bps since it got listed as an ETF.

Crucially, these fund managers are also optimizing the portfolio well to take on lower risks; note that the rolling volatility profile has been consistently lower than EWY’s (despite covering a much smaller pool of stocks) with the differential now working out to over 550bps!

One of the key differentiators here is that EWY appears to tilt very heavily towards the giant and large-caps (92% of the portfolio), with limited exposure to mid-caps and no exposure to small and micro-caps either. Note also that the average market-cap of EWY’s portfolio is over 2x higher than our focus product. While MKOR too has significant exposure to giant and large-caps (two-thirds of the portfolio), it also brings a healthy dose of small and micro-cap coverage, while mid-cap exposure is also quite respectable at 20%. A lot of these giant and large-cap stocks are more heavily oriented towards the forces of global markets, whereas Korea small and micro-caps are a better proxy of domestic conditions.

When it comes to other structural qualities, note that both products are quite top-heavy (the top 10 stocks account for over 50% of the total portfolio in both cases), but given that EWY covers a much wider pool of stocks (almost 3x more than MKOR) we thought it ought to have a much lower top 10 weight. What this suggests is that there may be quite a few stocks in EWY that are just making up the numbers and MKOR is more efficient with its stock picking.

Given that MKOR is an actively managed product, there’s no surprise to discover that it costs more than EWY (an expense ratio differential of 20bps), and also resorts to more churn (1.7x the level of annual churn that EWY witnesses). However, it more than makes up for this by a much superior yield equating to almost 5% (more than twice as high as EWY’s current yield of 2.26%).

Is MKOR A Good Buy Now?

As implied in the section above, given how difficult it has been for Korean equities by and large, MKOR has fared relatively well, while also offering a very tasty yield. Investors may also be interested to note that MKOR’s portfolio of stocks can be picked up at very compelling valuations relative to the globe. MKOR is priced at less than 10x P/E, which represents almost a 50% discount to the corresponding multiple of global stocks, as represented by the Vanguard Total World Stock ETF. Crucially, at such a steep P/E discount, MKOR’s holdings are also poised to deliver slightly superior long-term earnings growth of 11%.

In light of this mismatch, we feel that Korean stocks represent an ideal mean reversion bet for those looking at beaten down opportunities across the globe; as you can see from the image below, MKOR’s relative strength ratio versus the globe is around 19% off its mean

Having said all that, anyone contemplating a position in Korea now should keep in mind that trade-related commentary is likely to prompt plenty of volatility in these stocks.

Take a look at MKOR’s weekly developments since the end of last year. Broadly we can see that MKOR had been forming a trading range between the $22-$27 levels, but amidst some strong bearish pressure which kick-started in September (marked by a downward sloping red trendline), the ETF broke support in December, and likely looked on course for a long slump. Yet in recent weeks we’ve seen some good firefighting skills at the lower levels, with MKOR not only negating the trendline, but also recouping its old trading range. As things stand, within this trading range, the risk-reward for a long position looks favorable.

Coming back to tariffs, what’s a little heartening to note is that compared to most other trading partners of the US who remain vulnerable to reciprocal tariffs, South Korea is better positioned (even though it levies the second-highest tariff rates after India) as a free trade pact has obliterated the effect of this with only limited tariffs on rice. However, what could be particularly concerning is if the Trump administration starts playing around with non-tariff barriers such as VAT, taxes on digital services, etc. which may end up hurting South Korea.

Domestically, South Korea looks set to embark on a more fruitful journey, after a difficult period (GDP growth in Q4-24 only saw a 0.1% sequential increase) supported by the Bank of Korea’s (BoK) impetus to ease financing conditions. After holding rates steady in January, the market is budgeting for a 25bps cut this month. Meanwhile, consumer confidence is certainly not as resilient as before, but it has been moving higher for the last couple of months.

Similar improvements can also be seen with business confidence, which had been on a slump for six straight months, before posting an improvement this month.

All in all, we feel that despite the prospect of high volatility, the cheaply priced, and high-yielding MKOR may be worth a shot now.

data.title: L3Harris Technologies: Back In The Buy Zone Amid DOGE Woes (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I began accumulating shares of defense contractor L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX) in early 2023 when the market turned increasingly cautious about the rather bold acquisition of Aerojet Rocketdyne, especially given the comparatively high implied leverage and significant supply chain issues at the time. As the latter began to resolve and L3Harris management commented favorably on the Aerojet Rocketdyne integration in its subsequent earnings reports, LHX shares began to recover significantly - somewhat too quickly in my opinion.

In my last article, published in July 2024, I tempered my optimism for LHX stock in light of what, I thought, was quite a bit of advance praise. Since then, and especially after the election of Donald Trump and subsequent statements of intent from him and Elon Musk related to a significant cut in defense spending, L3Harris stock has let off quite a bit of steam. To date, LHX shares are down more than 25% from their - somewhat lofty - 52-week high.

With this in mind, I think it's time to re-evaluate the L3Harris Technologies investment case. In this update, I would like to present a fresh view on LHX stock, taking into account the company's recently released full-year 2024 results. Based on the updated fundamentals and management's outlook for 2025 and beyond, I will also provide a valuation update and conclude by explaining why I expect to add more LHX shares to my existing position.

L3Harris Technologies: Solid 2024 Results, And Focusing On What Matters Most

For 2024, L3Harris delivered adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $13.10, up 6% year-over-year and almost perfectly in line with estimates. Total revenues of $21.3 billion were also in line with estimates, up 10% year-over-year (+4% organically). Services revenues grew in line with total revenue, which is very encouraging (Figure 1, red). For 2025, management expects revenues of $22.0 billion (+/- $200 million), which translates to a rather subdued growth expectation of 3.2%. On slide 3 of the earnings presentation, management stated that they intend to achieve average annual organic revenue growth of 4% between 2023 and 2026, which implies growth of approximately 4.5% for 2026.

On a GAAP basis, earnings per share were significantly lower at only $7.87, which warrants a brief assessment of whether management's adjustments were appropriate.

The largest adjustment - 34% of non-GAAP EPS - resulted from the amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets and inventory value adjustments (see p. 17 and p. 19 of the earnings press release). Another $1.4 per share was due to the implementation of "LHX NeXt", the company's restructuring program (see this article for more details). While I have no reservations about the former adjustment, I am keeping an eye on potentially long-term recurring restructuring costs and watching how well the company meets the associated financial commitments.

Similarly, I would like to see the costs associated with mergers and acquisitions and divestitures gradually decline. Also, I believe L3Harris should stop acquiring companies and instead focus on more focused and increasingly profitable activities. M&A and divestiture-related losses and expenses represented a $0.83 per share headwind to 2024 EPS.

The results for 2024 show first signs of improvement, but it will of course still take some time before L3Harris can achieve an operating margin in the high teens. An adjusted margin of 15.4% was reported for 2024, 60 bps higher than in the previous year. Reported selling, general and administrative (SG&A) expenses decreased by 33 bps year-over-year to 16.7% of total revenues. In 2023, SG&A costs fell by 51 bps compared to 2022. In my opinion, these improvements are the result of good cost control, particularly in light of the ongoing restructuring.

Gross profitability weakened in 2024, at least on a reportable basis (-43 bps), but I found the significant recovery in the Services segment gross margin encouraging (Figure 2, red bars). However, I think that a return of the Services gross margin to the high 20% range is a critical aspect that management needs to pay a lot of attention to. Not only are service revenues more predictable and less cyclical than product revenues, but I also believe that this is where the majority of cash flows should ultimately be generated.

Looking at L3Harris' four reporting segments, the 8% year-over-year revenue growth in the Communications Systems segment is particularly noteworthy, as is the 50 bps improvement in the adjusted operating margin. In this context, it is worth noting that L3Harris is benefiting from increased demand from NATO member states in this segment, which should provide a continued tailwind. I do not currently attach too much weight to the possible withdrawal of the U.S. from NATO and the associated secondary effects under President Trump's second term in office - in either direction. Western Europe in particular will step up a gear in terms of defense spending, and here I see L3Harris well positioned, as well as larger defense companies from both the U.S. and Europe. At the same time, one could argue that the expected massive increase in the bloc's defense spending will need to be funded somehow, and this would likely result in a further weakening of the EUR against the USD - in my view mainly due to the expected funding via quantitative easing measures.

While the other segments - with the exception of Aerojet Rocketdyne - showed more muted revenue growth in 2024 (Figure 3), the 40bps (Space & Airborne Systems), 100bps (Integrated Mission Systems), and 90bps (Aerojet Rocketdyne) expansion in adjusted operating margin confirmed the progress in efficiency gains, although I would reiterate that the company still has a lot of work to do before I would consider profitability to be adequate.

Free cash flow in 2024 was fairly strong at around $2.3 billion on a reported basis, consistent with my own calculation, which includes working capital adjustments and the treatment of stock-based compensation as a cash expense. For 2025 and 2026, management projected $2.45 billion and $2.8 billion, respectively (Figure 4), however, excluding the impact of stock-based compensation (around 4%-5% of free cash flow). Note that Figure 4 also shows L3Harris' adjusted free cash flow margin (red line), which has returned to 2020-2021 levels in 2024. Taking into account management's guidance, free cash flow profitability is expected to be flat in the coming years (red line), which once again underlines that the company is in the midst of a multi-year restructuring program.

Given the solid - and growing - backlog (+4.6% year-over-year, Figure 5) and increasingly meaningful revenue visibility, I would argue that L3Harris management's free cash flow guidance can be trusted. Therefore, I will incorporate L3Harris' current adjusted free cash flow and management's guidance in the updated valuation discussed in the last part of the article.

Considering that L3Harris' 2024 10-K was filed last week, on February 14, it's a good time to take a fresh look at the company's financial stability as well. Long-term debt accounts for more than 90% of the company's total debt, almost all of which is fixed-rate debt. This shields L3Harris from changes in short-term interest rates (which typically form the basis for floating-rate debt coupon rates), but the company's ability to service its debt could still weaken in the event of refinancing transactions. Between 2025 and 2033, $8.4 billion will mature, spread relatively evenly over the years:

Refinancing a portion of the bonds at maturity would result in a decrease in L3Harris' interest coverage ratio, but not really much as the coupon of the maturing debt is already in the mid to high 4% range. Considering that the company generates annual free cash flow of around $800 million to $1.0 billion after dividends and share buybacks (averaging $500 million per year), and amid management's pledge to further reduce leverage from the current 2.9x net debt to adjusted EBITDA (slide 14 of the earnings presentation), I would argue that leverage is under control. In light of the gradually improving leverage following the acquisitions, I was not too surprised (but nevertheless pleased) that the rating agency Moody's affirmed L3Harris' Baa2 rating and changed the rating outlook from negative to stable.

Management's continued focus on achieving profitability targets and paying down debt (as well as a current increased emphasis on share buybacks) does not bode well for the dividend growth part of the LHX investment case, at least in theory and judging by recent history (Figure 7).

I want to make it clear that for me as a long-term investor, financial stability is a higher priority than high dividend growth, so I can overlook another year or two of low single-digit dividend increases. However, given management's free cash flow and payout ratio guidance for 2025 and 2026, I wouldn't rule out a somewhat more significant dividend increase later this week or early next week. After all, paying out 40% of free cash flow in 2025 and 2026 would represent an increase of 10% and 14%, respectively, based on the upper end of the guidance. During the earnings call, the company management emphasized its intention to "maintain a competitive dividend" and buy back shares worth at least $1 billion in 2025. Hopefully, share buybacks are reduced somewhat going into 2026, as this would leave sufficient cash flow on the table to further reduce debt.

Conclusion

Defense contractor L3Harris Technologies Inc. has delivered a solid 2024 earnings report that shows early signs that the company's restructuring program is taking hold. At the same time, I would argue that the company still has a lot of work to do, considering the significant acquisitions and also the still apparent overhang from the merger between Harris Corporation and L3 Technologies in mid-2019 and the pandemic lockdowns-related secondary effects.

The progress is illustrated, for example, by the $800 million in gross cost savings realized by 2024, which is twice as high as originally expected. While the significant cost reductions in a short period of time are indeed a sign that much can still be improved at L3Harris, I would argue that management is on the right track, especially in light of the $400 million of additional savings planned for 2025 ($200 million more than originally expected).

As a result of the gradual improvement in profitability, but also the recovery from the severe supply chain disruptions since the pandemic, free cash flow is expected to increase significantly in the coming years. The order backlog remains high, at around 1.6 times 2024 revenues at year-end, and the consolidation of Aerojet Rocketdyne gives management a much better view of longer-term revenues and therefore free cash flow.

All in all, I am positive that L3Harris can generate free cash flow of $2.4 billion to $2.7 billion in the coming years. Against this backdrop, I am increasingly confident that the company can reduce leverage while continuing to pay a (modestly) growing dividend and buy back around $500 million worth of shares each year. For 2025, twice this amount is expected, representing roughly 2.7% of the current market capitalization. Personally, I expect that dividend growth will remain in the low single digits for a few more years, given the still quite improvable profitability and the currently elevated leverage of 2.9x net debt to adjusted EBITDA. However, given the dividend growth guidance of up to 10% as implied by the expected free cash flow payout ratio, I look forward to next week with a little excitement, although I wouldn't rule out another $0.02 quarterly increase.

Factoring the free cash flow estimates - as well as pretty modest long-term expectations - into the discounted cash flow valuation model for LHX stock (Figure 8), I feel confident about adding more shares to the position I started building in early 2023. Based on a cost of equity of 8.5%, LHX shares would be worth $235 today, which, I believe, is a reasonable margin of safety. The FAST Graphs chart in Figure 9 also points to an increasingly compelling valuation, which currently stands at a blended price-to-earnings ratio of 15. I don't expect any significant headwinds from DOGE-related cost savings, as I believe these will be largely focused on overhead and not really on the actual defense products and related services. In fact, I wouldn't rule out the possibility that contractors like L3Harris might even benefit despite a modest cut in the defense budget due to materially reduced overhead.

Nonetheless, due to the comparatively weaker bargaining position with the Department of Defense, I intend to keep my L3Harris position smaller than my other defense holdings Lockheed Martin Corp. (LMT), RTX Corp. (RTX), Northrop Grumman Corp. (NOC) and more recently also General Dynamics Corp. (GD), which I expect to cover in an upcoming article.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.
